data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Harness can never strictly beat self-consistency on the same candidate pool with a single fixed-precision critic, because any filtering that removes incorrect candidates also removes correct candidates at a rate proportional to the critic's false-positive rate, and the net effect is always non-positive when the critic operates on the same distribution as the candidates. The observed strict-advantage in prior claims is an artifact of implicit pool modification (the critic effectively re-samples rather than filters). The decisive experiment: demonstrate that for ANY fixed critic precision P and recall R, there exists no base-error regime at any k where harness strictly beats SC by more than sampling noise.", "mechanism": "Consider the candidate pool as k draws from a distribution with error rate e. SC returns the majority. Harness applies a filter with precision P and recall R, then takes majority of survivors. If the filter reduces to <k/2 survivors, harness abstains or falls back to SC. The key theorem: for a critic trained on the same distribution, E[harness_accuracy] ≤ E[SC_accuracy] + O(1/sqrt(n)) for all e, k, P, R. Proof sketch: The critic's decisions are conditionally independent of candidate correctness given the pool's majority signal. When the majority is correct, false flags remove correct candidates, potentially flipping the majority. When the majority is incorrect, true flags remove incorrect candidates, potentially fixing it. But by Bayes' optimality on the shared distribution, the expected flip rate equals the expected fix rate (the critic cannot extract information not already in the majority). Any observed advantage is sampling variance.", "scope": {"covers": ["all k", "all base error rates", "critic trained/evaluated on same distribution as candidates", "single-round filtering (no iterative refinement)"], "excludes": ["critic with access to external information beyond candidate text (e.g., answer key, retrieval)", "iterative or multi-round criticism", "critic trained on a different/harder distribution than candidates"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "At k=7, e≈0.25, P>0.25, R>0.75: harness accuracy exceeds SC by >5pp with p<0.01 across 200 questions", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Net fixed-minus-broken > 0 in any single tested (e, k, P, R) cell with significance surviving Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "A critic with P>0.5, R>0.5 produces harness accuracy > SC accuracy at k=7, e=0.25 with lower 95% CI > 0", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "This is the null-hypothesis candidate: it claims the harness advantage is a statistical illusion. It directly negates the mechanism candidates. The forbidden cases are designed so that if ANY of them fires, this candidate is refuted and the mechanism candidates are supported. The experiment is the same as for the other candidates (targeted difficulty sampling at k=7), but the prediction is opposite. Budget: 200 questions × 7 candidates + 200 critic evaluations ≈ 1600 model calls, well within 300k tokens. This candidate is atypical because it contradicts the intuition that adversarial filtering should help, but it carries the strongest theoretical commitment and is most easily falsified."}
